worth_it_explanation: Blocking controls decide what programmatic demand is allowed to run on the publisher's pages, so they sit directly between brand safety and revenue. Settings left at defaults can let off-brand ads through, while blocks that are wider than they need to be starve demand and lower clearing prices. The audit is mostly a read of the Protections, advertiser, and ad technology settings against the publisher's own standards, and the fixes are configuration changes rather than rebuilds. Getting the balance right protects the reader experience and the rates the publisher's direct sales team has promised, while keeping competing demand as wide as the policy allows.
intro: Google Ad Manager (GAM) protections decide which programmatic ads are allowed to serve on a publisher's pages, through sensitive-category blocks, advertiser and URL block lists, and ad technology vendor controls. Settings that are too loose let off-brand ads through and can break a commitment made to a direct advertiser, while settings that are too tight remove competing demand and lower the price the publisher earns. These checks review the blocking controls as a balance between brand safety and revenue, confirming each block reflects a current, deliberate reason.

audit_checks.title: Sensitive category and ad content blocks are left at defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Default sensitive-category settings may not match the publisher's brand standards or a commitment made to a direct advertiser, so ads from categories the publisher would rather exclude can serve next to its content. The reverse also costs the publisher since blocking more categories than the brand standard requires removes demand and lowers the price the remaining demand clears at.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) lets a publisher block groups of ads that are classed as sensitive, such as alcohol, politics, religion, gambling, dating, or significant skin exposure, along with specific ad content. The blocks are created under 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', as an ad content protection that applies across the open auction, private auctions, and First Look. Google classifies the ads automatically rather than relying on what an advertiser declares, so the control is the publisher's main lever over the kinds of programmatic ads that appear beside its content.

The default settings are not the same as a deliberate brand-safety policy. A category the publisher would rather exclude can serve because it was never blocked, which puts an off-brand ad next to the content and can break a promise the direct sales team made to an advertiser about what runs near its placements. The reverse costs revenue. Every category blocked removes a slice of demand, so a block that is wider than the brand standard requires lowers the price the remaining demand clears at without protecting anything the publisher actually cares about.

Google does not guarantee that every ad in a sensitive category is caught since the classification is automatic and imperfect, so the block is a strong filter rather than a complete one. That makes it worth reading the current settings against the publisher's written standards rather than assuming the defaults reflect them.

A few signs point to the blocks needing review:
  • The sensitive-category settings are still at their defaults with no record of a brand-safety decision behind them
  • A direct advertiser was promised exclusions that are not reflected in the category blocks
  • Categories are blocked that the publisher's standards do not actually require, with no note of why
You may want to confirm the category blocks reflect a deliberate policy the publisher can point to, rather than settings that were never touched.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the sensitive-category settings are where the editorial brand meets the open auction, so a category that contradicts the publication's standards is worth blocking even at some revenue cost. You may want to map the publisher's written brand-safety standards to the available categories and confirm each is reflected, rather than relying on the defaults.
site_type_overlays.healthcare: On a health or medical publisher, categories such as weight loss, supplements, or alcohol can sit awkwardly next to clinical content and may conflict with the standards the audience expects. You may want to review those categories against the publisher's editorial policy before deciding which to allow.

investigate.intro: Open the ad content protections in Google Ad Manager and review the sensitive-category and ad-content settings against the publisher's brand-safety standards.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', and review the ad content protections that are in place.
Open or create the ad content protection and read the sensitive categories under 'Blocks', noting which categories are toggled on and which are left allowed.

Compare the blocked categories against the publisher's written brand-safety standards and any exclusions promised to direct advertisers. Note categories that should be blocked but are not, and categories blocked with no current reason behind them.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the sensitive-category and ad-content blocks that are configured and compares them against the publisher's brand-safety standards and any advertiser commitments. A set of blocks that reflects a deliberate policy, with the categories the standard requires blocked and no unexplained extra blocks, passes. Defaults left in place with no decision behind them, a missing block the standard calls for, or a block wider than the standard requires is a fail recorded with the category at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the configured sensitive-category blocks and the publisher's brand-safety standard above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the sensitive-category and ad content blocks on {{site_name}}'s Google Ad Manager (GAM) inventory are left at or near their defaults rather than set to a deliberate brand-safety policy. Default settings can let off-brand ads serve beside the content and can miss exclusions promised to direct advertisers, while blocks wider than the brand standard needs remove demand and lower clearing prices. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the publisher's written brand-safety standards to the available categories, blocking the categories the standard calls for, and removing blocks that no current standard supports. After the change, confirm the protection reflects the policy the publisher can point to.

audit_checks.title: URL and advertiser block lists are stale or broader than needed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Advertiser and URL block lists grow as one-off decisions accumulate, and entries that no longer apply keep removing competing demand without a brand-safety reason that still holds. Fewer bidders in the auction means lower bid density, which lowers the price the publisher's inventory clears at.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) lets a publisher block specific advertisers, brands, and destination URLs so their ads do not serve on the publisher's inventory. The blocks live under 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', and they apply across the open auction, First Look, and programmatic guaranteed depending on how each is set. The lists are a precise tool for keeping a named competitor or an unwanted destination off the pages.

The lists grow over time as one-off decisions get added and rarely removed. A brand blocked for a campaign that ended a year ago, a competitor that is no longer relevant, or a URL added during a single incident all stay on the list until someone reviews it. Each entry that no longer applies keeps a bidder out of the auction without a brand-safety reason that still holds.

That carries a revenue cost. Every advertiser or URL on the block list is demand the publisher's inventory cannot clear against, so a list that is wider than it needs to be lowers bid density and the price the auction settles at. The cost is invisible in the protections view itself since a stale block looks the same as a current one, which is why it helps to read each entry against a reason that still applies today.

A few signs point to a list that needs trimming:
  • Blocks tied to campaigns or incidents that have since ended, with no note that the reason still applies
  • Advertisers or brands blocked years ago that no longer compete with or threaten the publisher
  • A block list long enough that no one can say why each entry is on it
You may want to review the advertiser and URL block lists for entries that are stale or wider than the brand-safety reason requires, keeping only the blocks with a current reason behind them.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, competitor and category blocks accumulate from years of direct-sales relationships, some of which have ended. You may want to confirm each advertiser or brand block still reflects a relationship or standard that applies, rather than one carried over from a campaign that has since wrapped.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher, URL and advertiser blocks are often added to keep direct competitors off the pages, and that competitive set shifts as the retail mix changes. You may want to review the blocks against the publisher's current competitors rather than the set that was relevant when the list was first built.

investigate.intro: Open the advertiser and URL block lists in Google Ad Manager and review each entry for whether its reason still applies.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', and find the protections that block specific advertisers, brands, or destination URLs.
Read through the blocked advertisers and brands, noting any tied to a campaign, relationship, or incident that has since ended.
Read through the blocked URLs the same way, noting entries that no longer match a current competitor or concern.
For each entry, record whether a current reason still applies or whether it looks stale and could be removed to widen the available demand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each advertiser, brand, and URL block alongside whether a current reason still supports it. A list where every active block maps to a reason that applies today passes. Any block tied to an ended campaign or relationship, or an entry no one can explain, is a fail recorded with the entry and the reason it appears stale.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the advertiser and URL block list entries and a current reason for each above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser and URL block lists on {{site_name}}'s Google Ad Manager (GAM) inventory carry entries that look stale or wider than the brand-safety reason behind them requires. Each block that no longer applies keeps a bidder out of the auction, which lowers bid density and the price the inventory clears at, with no brand-safety benefit in return. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the advertiser and URL block lists, confirming a current reason for each entry, and removing the blocks that no longer apply. After the change, confirm every remaining block maps to a reason that holds today.

audit_checks.title: Allowed ad technology vendors are not reviewed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The ad technology provider controls decide which third-party vendors may serve, measure, and collect data on the publisher's inventory. An unreviewed list can allow vendors the publisher did not intend, which is both a brand-safety exposure and a privacy exposure since those vendors operate on the publisher's users in regions covered by consent rules.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) gives a publisher controls over which ad technology providers, the third-party vendors that handle measurement, verification, and data collection, may operate on its inventory. There are two related surfaces. The ad technologies control under 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', governs which certified vendors are allowed to serve and measure, and the ad technology partner (ATP) controls govern which partners may serve and measure ads for users in the European Economic Area (EEA), the United Kingdom, and Switzerland under consent rules.

Leaving these settings unreviewed can let vendors operate that the publisher did not choose. Google notes that if a publisher does not engage with the ATP controls, a commonly used set of partners serves by default, so a vendor the publisher would not have picked can run on its users without a deliberate decision behind it. That is a brand-safety question since the vendor's behavior reflects on the publisher's pages, and a privacy question since the vendor collects data from the publisher's users.

The privacy side carries its own weight. Google states that building a vendor list which meets IAB Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) and General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) transparency requirements is the publisher's responsibility, so an unreviewed ATP list is a compliance gap as well as a brand-safety one. A vendor allowed by default still has to be one the publisher can stand behind in its consent flow.

A few signs point to the vendor controls needing a review:
  • The ATP controls were never engaged, so the default partner set is serving rather than a chosen one
  • The allowed ad technologies include vendors with no record of why they were permitted
  • The vendor list has not been reconciled against the publisher's consent flow and privacy commitments
You may want to review the ad technologies and ATP controls and confirm the allowed vendor set is one the publisher chose on purpose.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that draws readers across the European Economic Area and the United Kingdom, the ad technology partner list feeds directly into the consent message readers see, so an unreviewed list can surface vendors the publisher cannot explain. You may want to reconcile the allowed partners against the consent management platform's vendor list and the publisher's privacy commitments.
site_type_overlays.healthcare: On a health publisher, the data third-party vendors collect from readers can be more sensitive than on a general site, so the allowed vendor set deserves a closer review. You may want to confirm each permitted ad technology provider is one the publisher is comfortable operating on health-related content.

investigate.intro: Open the ad technologies and ad technology partner controls in Google Ad Manager and confirm the allowed vendors are ones the publisher chose deliberately.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', and find the ad technologies control. Note which certified vendors are allowed to serve and measure.
Open the ad technology partner (ATP) controls in the account settings and note whether the controls were engaged or whether the default commonly used partner set is serving.
Compare the allowed vendors and partners against the publisher's intended vendor list and its consent management platform's vendor selections.
Record any vendor or partner that is allowed with no record of why, and any gap between the allowed set and the publisher's consent flow.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the allowed ad technologies and ATP partners and whether each reflects a deliberate choice the publisher can explain. A vendor set that the publisher chose on purpose and that reconciles with its consent flow passes. Default partners serving because the controls were never engaged, or an allowed vendor with no reason behind it, is a fail recorded with the vendor and the gap it represents.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the allowed ad technologies and ATP partners and whether each was chosen deliberately above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ad technology provider controls on {{site_name}}'s Google Ad Manager (GAM) inventory have not been reviewed, so the allowed vendor set may not reflect a deliberate choice. When the ad technology partner (ATP) controls are left unengaged, a default set of partners serves and collects data from the publisher's users, which is both a brand-safety exposure and a privacy exposure under consent rules. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the ad technologies and ATP controls, confirming each allowed vendor is one the publisher chose, and reconciling the list against the consent flow and privacy commitments. After the change, confirm the allowed vendor set is one the publisher can stand behind.
